_meta.source: CRPD Committee (Committee on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities)
_meta.note: General Comments are not formally binding treaty law but constitute authoritative interpretation of CRPD obligations by the treaty body. They are primary analytical inputs in the NormTrace conventionality analysis stage.

general_comments.title: Article 12: Equal recognition before the law
key_holdings: All persons with disabilities have full legal capacity; legal capacity cannot be restricted on the basis of disability, whether status-based or functional.
The functional approach (assessing mental capacity to make specific decisions) is incompatible with Art. 12; it discriminates on the basis of disability.
Supported decision-making must replace all forms of substituted decision-making (guardianship, tutela, curatela, interdiction, conservatorship).

Support arrangements must: respect the rights, will and preferences of the person; be free of conflict of interest and undue influence; be proportional and tailored to the person's circumstances; apply for the shortest time possible; be subject to regular review by an independent, impartial and accessible authority.
The obligation to replace substituted decision-making with supported decision-making is immediately applicable, not subject to progressive realisation.
Art. 12 is inherently linked to Art. 14 (liberty and security), Art. 19 (independent living), and Art. 29 (political participation).
abrogates: Medical model of disability in legal capacity law
Functional capacity assessments as basis for capacity restriction

general_comments.title: Article 9: Accessibility
key_holdings: Accessibility is a precondition for the equal enjoyment of all CRPD rights; it must be treated as a systemic obligation, not a case-by-case accommodation.
Accessibility obligations are immediately applicable; they cannot be relegated to progressive realisation.
Accessibility applies to both public and private actors providing public services, goods and facilities.
The obligation covers: physical environment; transportation; information and communications (including ICT and emergency systems); facilities open to or provided for the public.
States must establish and enforce minimum accessibility standards with monitoring, complaint mechanisms, and effective sanctions.
Reasonable accommodation (individual) and accessibility (systemic) are distinct obligations; one does not substitute for the other.
Universal design is the primary methodology; accessibility retrofitting is a secondary and insufficient response.
Rural and remote areas are not exempt from accessibility requirements.

general_comments.title: Article 6: Women and girls with disabilities
key_holdings: Women and girls with disabilities experience multiple and intersectional discrimination; this is a distinct legal category requiring targeted measures.
Intersectionality analysis requires examining the simultaneous interaction of gender and disability, not additive analysis of separate grounds.
Gender-neutral disability law fails to recognise or address multiple discrimination; states must integrate gender and disability policy frameworks.
Violence against women with disabilities — including domestic violence, sexual violence, forced sterilisation, and institutional violence — requires specific legislative recognition and remedy.
States must ensure women with disabilities have access to gender-based violence support services in accessible formats.
Forced sterilisation of women with disabilities constitutes a violation of Arts. 6, 17 (integrity of person), and 23 (family).
Disabled women must have equal access to reproductive health services and decision-making.
Data collection must be disaggregated by both gender and disability to make multiple discrimination visible.

general_comments.title: Article 24: Right to inclusive education
key_holdings: Inclusive education means transformation of the general education system so that it can accommodate all learners; it is not synonymous with integration (placement without systemic change).
Segregated special education as a primary model is incompatible with Art. 24; it constitutes structural discrimination.
The 'right to be welcomed in the regular school' is absolute: no child with a disability may be excluded from general education on the basis of disability.
Reasonable accommodation in education is a non-discrimination duty: denial of accommodation is discrimination.
Dual systems (inclusive and segregated) that direct children to special education on the basis of disability violate Art. 24.
Teachers must be trained in inclusive methodologies; lack of training does not justify exclusion.

Quality inclusive education requires: physical accessibility of schools; accessible information and communication; trained teachers; individualised support; involvement of parents and students in decision-making.
The obligation to move toward inclusive education is immediate (non-discrimination) and progressive (full realisation); no regression is permitted.

general_comments.title: Article 19: Living independently and being included in the community
key_holdings: Deinstitutionalisation is an obligation, not a policy option; institutionalisation violates the right to community living regardless of institution size, quality, or voluntary admission.
The right to choose where and with whom to live is absolute; no person may be compelled to live in a particular living arrangement on the basis of disability.
States must invest in community-based support services as an alternative to institutional care; progressive resource reallocation from institutions to community support is required.
Personal assistance services are a specific right under Art. 19(b); they must be personalised, portable, and controlled by the person with disability.

The duty to deinstitutionalise encompasses: mental health institutions, long-term care facilities, group homes replicating institutional logic, and family-home care models that lack individual support.
Family-only care models do not satisfy Art. 19 when they are imposed by structural absence of state support.
The Convention does not permit 'better institutions' as a compliance strategy; community living is the standard.
Art. 19 generates cross-cutting obligations linking to accessibility (Art. 9), independent living support, and social protection (Art. 28).

general_comments.title: Article 5: Equality and non-discrimination
key_holdings: Four components of equality are required: formal (equal treatment), substantive (different treatment that corrects disadvantage), transformative (structural change), and participatory (equal voice in law and policy).
Denial of reasonable accommodation constitutes discrimination; it is an immediately applicable obligation, not subject to progressive realisation.

Multiple and intersectional discrimination are distinct legal concepts: multiple = two or more grounds in aggregate; intersectional = grounds that interact to produce a specific, combined form of discrimination.
Structural discrimination — laws, policies, and practices that perpetuate disadvantage — requires affirmative measures, not just prohibition of overt discrimination.

Disability cannot be an implicit or explicit criterion for different treatment unless it constitutes a proportionate, necessary, and legitimate differentiation designed to achieve substantive equality.
Reasonable accommodation must be assessed individually; a general policy cannot serve as a substitute.
Refusal to make accommodation without engaging in the interactive process constitutes discrimination.
The duty to provide reasonable accommodation extends to all domains covered by the CRPD, including all areas regulated by states parties.
